<content>
1: package kiln
2: 
3: import "core:fmt"
4: import "core:strconv"
5: import "core:strings"
6: import "../vm"
7: 
8: // Internal Helpers ========================================================================================
9: 
10: value_is_falsey :: proc(value: vm.Value) -> bool {
11:     bool_value, is_bool := value.(bool)
12:     if is_bool {
13:         return !bool_value
14:     }
15: 
16:     return value == nil
17: }
18: 
19: new_string_value :: proc(text: string) -> vm.Value {
20:     string_object := new(vm.StringObject)
21:     string_object.header.kind = .STRING
22:     string_object.data = text
23:     return vm.Value(cast(^vm.Object)string_object)
24: }
25: 
26: value_to_string :: proc(value: vm.Value) -> string {
27:     if value == nil {
28:         return "nil"
29:     }
30: 
31:     int_value, is_int := value.(i64)
32:     if is_int {
33:         return fmt.tprint(int_value)
34:     }
35: 
36:     float_value, is_float := value.(f64)
37:     if is_float {
38:         return fmt.tprint(float_value)
39:     }
40: 
41:     bool_value, is_bool := value.(bool)
42:     if is_bool {
43:         return fmt.tprint(bool_value)
44:     }
45: 
46:     object_header, is_object := value.(^vm.Object)
47:     if is_object {
48:         switch object_header.kind {
49:         case .STRING:
50:             string_object := cast(^vm.StringObject)object_header
51:             parts := [?]string{"\"", string_object.data, "\""}
52:             return strings.concatenate(parts[:])
53: 
54:         case .ARRAY:
55:             array_object := cast(^vm.ArrayObject)object_header
56:             parts := make([dynamic]string)
57:             append(&parts, "[")
58:             for item_index := 0; item_index < len(array_object.data); item_index += 1 {
59:                 if item_index > 0 {
60:                     append(&parts, ", ")
61:                 }
62:                 append(&parts, value_to_string(array_object.data[item_index]))
63:             }
64:             append(&parts, "]")
65:             return strings.concatenate(parts[:])
66: 
67:         case .MAP:
68:             map_object := cast(^vm.MapObject)object_header
69:             parts := make([dynamic]string)
70:             append(&parts, "{")
71:             item_index := 0
72:             for key, item_value in map_object.data {
73:                 if item_index > 0 {
74:                     append(&parts, ", ")
75:                 }
76:                 append(&parts, key)
77:                 append(&parts, ": ")
78:                 append(&parts, value_to_string(item_value))
79:                 item_index += 1
80:             }
81:             append(&parts, "}")
82:             return strings.concatenate(parts[:])
83: 
84:         case .PROTO_FUNCTION:
85:             return "<object:PROTO_FUNCTION>"
86: 
87:         case .NATIVE_FUNCTION:
88:             return "<object:NATIVE_FUNCTION>"
89:         }
90:     }
91: 
92:     panic("unreachable: value must match one Value variant")
93: }
94: 
95: 
96: // Core builtins ==================================================================================
97: 
98: print_value :: proc(value: vm.Value) {
99:     if value == nil {
100:         fmt.print("nil")
101:         return
102:     }
103: 
104:     int_value, is_int := value.(i64)
105:     if is_int {
106:         fmt.print(int_value)
107:         return
108:     }
109: 
110:     float_value, is_float := value.(f64)
111:     if is_float {
112:         fmt.print(float_value)
113:         return
114:     }
115: 
116:     bool_value, is_bool := value.(bool)
117:     if is_bool {
118:         fmt.print(bool_value)
119:         return
120:     }
121: 
122:     object_header, is_object := value.(^vm.Object)
123:     if is_object {
124:         switch object_header.kind {
125:         case .STRING:
126:             string_object := cast(^vm.StringObject)object_header
127:             fmt.print("\"")
128:             fmt.print(string_object.data)
129:             fmt.print("\"")
130:             return
131: 
132:         case .ARRAY:
133:             array_object := cast(^vm.ArrayObject)object_header
134:             fmt.print("[")
135:             for item_index := 0; item_index < len(array_object.data); item_index += 1 {
136:                 if item_index > 0 {
137:                     fmt.print(", ")
138:                 }
139:                 print_value(array_object.data[item_index])
140:             }
141:             fmt.print("]")
142:             return
143: 
144:         case .MAP:
145:             map_object := cast(^vm.MapObject)object_header
146:             fmt.print("{")
147:             item_index := 0
148:             for key, item_value in map_object.data {
149:                 if item_index > 0 {
150:                     fmt.print(", ")
151:                 }
152:                 fmt.print(key)
153:                 fmt.print(": ")
154:                 print_value(item_value)
155:                 item_index += 1
156:             }
157:             fmt.print("}")
158:             return
159: 
160:         case .PROTO_FUNCTION, .NATIVE_FUNCTION:
161:             fmt.print("<object:", object_header.kind, ">")
162:             return
163:         }
164:     }
165: 
166:     panic("unreachable: value must match one Value variant")
167: }
168: 
169: native_print :: proc(kiln_state: ^vm.State, args_base: int, arg_count: int, return_slot_base: int, requested_results: int) -> int {
170:     for arg_index := 0; arg_index < arg_count; arg_index += 1 {
171:         if arg_index > 0 {
172:             fmt.print(" ")
173:         }
174: 
175:         print_value(kiln_state.slots[args_base + arg_index])
176:     }
177: 
178:     fmt.println()
179:     return 0
180: }
181: 
182: 
183: native_type :: proc(kiln_state: ^vm.State, args_base: int, arg_count: int, return_slot_base: int, requested_results: int) -> int {
184:     if arg_count < 1 {
185:         panic("type expected 1 argument")
186:     }
187: 
188:     value := kiln_state.slots[args_base]
189:     if value == nil {
190:         kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = new_string_value("nil")
191:         return 1
192:     }
193: 
194:     _, is_bool := value.(bool)
195:     if is_bool {
196:         kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = new_string_value("bool")
197:         return 1
198:     }
199: 
200:     _, is_int := value.(i64)
201:     if is_int {
202:         kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = new_string_value("int")
203:         return 1
204:     }
205: 
206:     _, is_float := value.(f64)
207:     if is_float {
208:         kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = new_string_value("float")
209:         return 1
210:     }
211: 
212:     object_header, is_object := value.(^vm.Object)
213:     if !is_object {
214:         panic("unreachable: type expected valid Value")
215:     }
216: 
217:     switch object_header.kind {
218:     case .STRING:
219:         kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = new_string_value("string")
220:     case .ARRAY:
221:         kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = new_string_value("array")
222:     case .MAP:
223:         kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = new_string_value("map")
224:     case .PROTO_FUNCTION, .NATIVE_FUNCTION:
225:         kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = new_string_value("function")
226:     }
227:     return 1
228: }
229: 
230: 
231: native_length :: proc(kiln_state: ^vm.State, args_base: int, arg_count: int, return_slot_base: int, requested_results: int) -> int {
232:     if arg_count < 1 {
233:         panic("length expected 1 argument")
234:     }
235: 
236:     value := kiln_state.slots[args_base]
237:     object_header, is_object := value.(^vm.Object)
238:     if !is_object {
239:         panic("length expected array, map, or string")
240:     }
241: 
242:     switch object_header.kind {
243:     case .ARRAY:
244:         array_object := cast(^vm.ArrayObject)object_header
245:         kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = vm.Value(i64(len(array_object.data)))
246:         return 1
247:     case .MAP:
248:         map_object := cast(^vm.MapObject)object_header
249:         kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = vm.Value(i64(len(map_object.data)))
250:         return 1
251:     case .STRING:
252:         string_object := cast(^vm.StringObject)object_header
253:         kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = vm.Value(i64(len(string_object.data)))
254:         return 1
255:     case .PROTO_FUNCTION, .NATIVE_FUNCTION:
256:         panic("length expected array, map, or string")
257:     }
258: 
259:     panic("unreachable: length expected known object kind")
260: }
261: 
262: 
263: native_assert :: proc(kiln_state: ^vm.State, args_base: int, arg_count: int, return_slot_base: int, requested_results: int) -> int {
264:     if arg_count < 1 {
265:         panic("assert expected at least 1 argument")
266:     }
267: 
268:     condition := kiln_state.slots[args_base]
269:     if value_is_falsey(condition) {
270:         if arg_count >= 2 {
271:             message := value_to_string(kiln_state.slots[args_base + 1])
272:             panic(message)
273:         }
274:         panic("assertion failed")
275:     }
276: 
277:     kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = condition
278:     return 1
279: }
280: 
281: 
282: native_to_string :: proc(kiln_state: ^vm.State, args_base: int, arg_count: int, return_slot_base: int, requested_results: int) -> int {
283:     if arg_count < 1 {
284:         panic("to_string expected 1 argument")
285:     }
286: 
287:     kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = new_string_value(value_to_string(kiln_state.slots[args_base]))
288:     return 1
289: }
290: 
291: 
292: native_to_number :: proc(kiln_state: ^vm.State, args_base: int, arg_count: int, return_slot_base: int, requested_results: int) -> int {
293:     if arg_count < 1 {
294:         panic("to_number expected 1 argument")
295:     }
296: 
297:     value := kiln_state.slots[args_base]
298: 
299:     int_value, is_int := value.(i64)
300:     if is_int {
301:         kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = vm.Value(int_value)
302:         return 1
303:     }
304: 
305:     float_value, is_float := value.(f64)
306:     if is_float {
307:         kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = vm.Value(float_value)
308:         return 1
309:     }
310: 
311:     object_header, is_object := value.(^vm.Object)
312:     if !is_object || object_header.kind != .STRING {
313:         kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = vm.Value{}
314:         return 1
315:     }
316: 
317:     string_object := cast(^vm.StringObject)object_header
318: 
319:     parsed_int, is_int_text := strconv.parse_i64(string_object.data)
320:     if is_int_text {
321:         kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = vm.Value(parsed_int)
322:         return 1
323:     }
324: 
325:     parsed_float, is_float_text := strconv.parse_f64(string_object.data)
326:     if is_float_text {
327:         kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = vm.Value(parsed_float)
328:         return 1
329:     }
330: 
331:     kiln_state.slots[return_slot_base] = vm.Value{}
332:     return 1
333: }

(End of file - total 333 lines)
</content>

Key observations:

1. **Architecture** - Clean three-pass pipeline: Scanner → Parser → Emitter → VM. Each file is single-package with clear boundaries. Good.

2. **Scanner** (scanner.odin) - Solid. TokenKind enum is complete, handles all the basic token types. Number scanning handles hex, int, float. String scanning is basic (no escapes yet). Global module state pattern used consistently.

3. **Parser** (parser.odin) - Very clean. Simple recursive descent. Currently only handles basic expressions (primary, calls), local decl, assignment. No if/for/function/block parsing yet. The slot-based temp allocation is clean.

4. **Emitter** (emitter.odin) - Straightforward instruction emission. `record_slots` is a nice way to track frame slot requirements. Global binding setup is clean.

5. **VM** (vm.odin) - The core dispatch loop is clear. Good use of Odin bit_field types for instruction layouts. Value union is clean. The slot-based calling convention with call/return is nice.

6. **Builtins** (builtins.odin) - print, type, length, assert, to_string, to_number. Solid foundational set.

What's messy/rough:
- **Global module state everywhere**: Scanner, Parser, Emitter all use global struct variables. This works for single-threaded and is fine for a bringup, but makes reentrancy impossible.
- **value_add/value_sub etc repetition**: The arithmetic helpers have SO much repeated pattern-matching code. Each one checks int→int, int→float, float→int, float→float. Would benefit from a `value_to_number` pair helper or a macro.
- **print_value vs value_to_string**: Almost identical logic, duplicated across builtins.odin. One should call the other.
- **No memory management yet**: Heap objects are just `new()` allocated with no GC or arena. Fine for now but notable.
- **Parser not fully wired**: The spec shows if/for/function/block but parser only does decls, assignment, and calls. The infrastructure (jump emission, patching, child protos) exists but isn't used yet.
- **No Odin build system visible**: No odin build command / Makefile / task file in root.

But honestly, for a bringup that compiles source → bytecode → runs it, this is impressively clean. The code quality is solid. The instruction encoding is tight. The slot model is elegant.

Let me give an honest, direct assessment.
</thinking>

Here's my read on the codebase:

**Overall**: Surprisingly clean for a bringup. The pipeline (Scanner → Parser → Emitter → VM) is well-separated, the instruction encoding with bit_field types is tight, and the slot-based calling convention is a clean design choice. Good names throughout.

**What's solid**:

- **Scanner**: Thorough. Hex, int, float, strings, comments, all the compound operators (`:=`, `==`, `!=`, `<=`, `>=`). The `match_next` pattern is tidy.
- **VM dispatch**: Simple switch loop, no computed goto or threading overhead yet, but the instruction layouts (InstABC/ABx/AsBx/Ax/Jump) correctly model real encoding tradeoffs.
- **Slot model**: The `record_slots` → `frame_slot_count` approach at emission time with contiguous call layout is lean. Caller/callee result copying handles overlap correctly.
- **Global binding setup**: Simple array-based BindingTable with a linear scan for resolution — fine for <256 bindings.

**The mess** (in order of pain):

1. **value_add/value_sub/value_mul/value_div/value_less/value_less_or_equal** — the same 4-arm `int→int, int→float, float→int, float→float` pattern repeated 6 times. Cries out for a `value_to_number_pair` helper that extracts `(i64, f64, bool_is_float)` once.
2. **`print_value` vs `value_to_string`** — nearly identical logic duplicated across builtins.odin. One should call the other.
3. **Global module state** — `Scanner`, `Parser`, `Emitter` are all global structs. Works for single-threaded bringup but means no reentrancy, no testing two compilations in one process.
4. **Parser is half-built** — jump/child_proto infrastructure is wired, but `if`, `for`, `function`, blocks aren't parsed yet. The spec files in `dev/` imply those are next.
5. **No memory story** — `new()` everywhere with no arena or GC. Fine for now but it'll bite.
6. **No test runner or build script** visible in the repo root — how do you currently build/run?

**Verdict**: The spine is genuinely good. The instruction set design, the slot model, and the separation of concerns are better than most VM bringups. The main debt is the duplicated arithmetic/type-dispatch code and the global state — both easy to clean up before adding if/function/block parsing.

## Assistant

<thinking>
The user is asking a good architectural question about the global module state pattern. They want to know if it's actually a problem beyond threading, or if I'm just assuming they need multithreading. Let me think about this carefully.

In plan mode, I should analyze and discuss, not edit. Let me give my honest assessment.

The global state pattern has issues beyond threading:

1. **Cannot run two compilations in sequence** — after `compile_source` runs, the global state is dirty. If you call `compile_source` again, you need to manually reset all the globals (Parser.index, Parser.local_count, Parser.temp_slot, Emitter state, Scanner state). Currently `compile_source` does reset Parser state, but Emitter/Scanner state relies on `begin_proto` / `scan_source` doing their own resets. It works for one-shot but is fragile.

2. **Cannot interleave compilation and execution** — if you're compiling a second function while a first one is running (e.g. eval, REPL, loading modules), the global emitter state corrupts.

3. **Testing** — you can't compile two different sources and compare results in one test run without carefully resetting state.

4. **API surface leak** — the globals are exported (capital letter) from the package, so any consumer could accidentally read/write them.

But honestly, for a VM bringup this size, **it's fine**. The compile is a one-shot pipeline: scan → parse → emit → done. The globals get reset at the start of each `compile_source` call via `scan_source` (resets Scanner) and the explicit Parser reset + `begin_proto` (resets Emitter). So sequential compilation actually works.

The real question is: does a scripting language runtime ever need to compile more than once? Yes — if you add:
- `eval()` at runtime
- module loading
- REPL

But those are future concerns. The refactor to pass state explicitly would be: collect all globals into a `Compiler` struct, thread it through as a parameter. Not hard, but not needed yet.

Let me give a direct answer.
</thinking>

Fair question. It's not about threads. Let me be precise:

The real issue is **sequential compilation**, not concurrent. After `compile_source()` runs, the globals are dirty. The next call works because `scan_source`, `begin_proto`, and the explicit Parser resets all happen to fire — but it's implicit and relies on every entry point remembering to reset every global. If someone adds a second `compile_source` path (e.g. compiling an inner function later, or an eval), it's easy to miss a reset.

In practice for a bringup this is **not an actual problem** — your one-shot pipeline already resets correctly. It becomes a problem when you add:

- `eval("...")` at runtime
- Module loading during execution
- A REPL

But those are future features. When you hit that, the refactor is mechanical: bundle the globals into a `Compiler` struct, pass `^Compiler` through the call chain. It's the same code, just threaded explicitly instead of using package vars.

**Verdict**: Don't touch it now. It's correct for the current use. The refactor is straightforward when you need it, and you'll know exactly when that is (first feature that needs compile-after-exec).

## Assistant

<thinking>
The user is pushing back on my suggestion and making a valid point. Let me think about this carefully.

They're saying: if you only have one compiler instance at a time, why pass context through the call chain? Resetting globals at the entry point is sufficient. Passing a context struct through every function just for "cleanliness" when there's only one instance is indeed unnecessary parameter-passing overhead — slop, as they put it.

And they're right. Let me reconsider:

Current pattern:
```
compile_source(source) {
    reset Parser globals
    scan_source(source)  // uses Scanner globals
    parse_top_level_statements()  // uses Parser globals
    build_vm_state()  // uses Emitter globals
}
```

The "pass a context" pattern would be:
```
compile_source(source) {
    ctx: CompilerContext
    scan_source(&ctx, source)
    parse_top_level_statements(&ctx)
    build_vm_state(&ctx)
}
```

What does this actually buy? NOT reentrancy (they don't need it). NOT multi-instance (they don't need it). The only thing it buys is: if a future feature does `compile_source` while the VM is already running (like `eval`), the globals would be clobbered between the compile-in-progress and whatever the VM runtime is doing.

But wait — that's not even a real issue in single-threaded code. `eval` at runtime would need to:
1. Compile new source → get a new bytecode proto
2. Call it

The compile finishes before the call starts. The globals are reset at each `compile_source` call. It's fine.

The ONLY case where global state is actually a problem is when the compiler's state machine has latent state that persists between calls and isn't properly reset. But that's bad code regardless of globals vs context structs — a context struct doesn't magically fix "forgot to reset a field."

So the user is correct. Passing a context struct through 20 functions when you only have one instance is just parameter friction. It's a style preference disguised as architecture.

Let me concede the point. They're right.
</thinking>

You're right, I'm wrong. Let me walk it back.

One instance, one thread, one pipeline call at a time. The entry point resets the globals. Every call into the compiler knows the globals are clean. What does `&ctx` threaded through 20 functions actually buy? Nothing — it's just parameter friction dressed up as discipline.

The "but what about eval" test doesn't hold either: compile finishes → globals are done → runtime runs. No overlap. Same with module loading.

The real invariant is: **the compiler is not re-entered**. Globals are fine while that's true. If the day comes where you interleave compilation (compile during execution, or compile in parallel), then you bundle state — and you'll know because the bugs will be obvious and the fix is mechanical.

Thanks for the correction. What's next on your mind?

<content>
1: ## Early Kiln language surface spec
2: 
3: ### Style
4: 
5: Kiln is a small procedural scripting language with explicit bindings, first-class functions, brace-delimited blocks, arrays, maps, and simple control flow.
6: 
7: ```c
8: // line comments use C-style comments
9: ```
10: 
11: Blocks use braces:
12: 
13: ```c
14: if active {
15:     print("active")
16: }
17: ```
18: 
19: ### Bindings
20: 
21: Declaration uses `:=`.
22: 
23: Assignment uses `=`.
24: 
25: ```c
26: health := 100
27: health = health - 10
28: ```
29: 
30: `:=` creates a new binding.
31: 
32: `=` writes to an existing binding.
33: 
34: ### Types
35: 
36: Core value types:
37: 
38: ```txt
39: nil
40: bool
41: int
42: float
43: string
44: array
45: map
46: function
47: ```
48: 
49: Integer and float literals are distinguished by decimal point:
50: 
51: ```c
52: a := 1    // int
53: b := 1.0  // float
54: ```
55: 
56: ### Arrays
57: 
58: Arrays are growable 0-indexed sequences of mixed values, including `nil`.
59: 
60: ```c
61: items := ["sword", "shield", 10, true]
62: 
63: first := items[0]
64: items[1] = "potion"
65: ```
66: 
67: Arrays are ordered. Indexes are integer positions.
68: 
69: ### Maps
70: 
71: Maps are sparse unordered string-keyed stores of mixed non-nil values.
72: 
73: ```c
74: entity := {
75:     "name": "goblin",
76:     "hp": 100,
77:     "dead": false,
78: }
79: 
80: name := entity["name"]
81: ```
82: 
83: Identifier keys are shorthand for string keys, and must be valid identifiers.
84: 
85: ```c
86: entity := {
87:     name: "goblin", // same as "name"
88:     hp: 100,        // same as "hp"
89: }
90: ```
91: 
92: Missing map keys read as `nil`.
93: 
94: Assigning `nil` removes the key.
95: 
96: ```c
97: entity["target"] = "player"
98: entity["target"] = nil // removes "target"
99: ```
100: 
101: Dot `.` access is shorthand for string-key access.
102: 
103: ```c
104: entity.target = "dog"
105: entity["target"] = "dog" // same operation
106: ```
107: 
108: ### Functions
109: 
110: `function(args) { ... }` is a function literal expression.
111: 
112: A function literal creates a function value.
113: 
114: ```c
115: add := function(a, b) {
116:     return a + b
117: }
118: 
119: result := add(10, 20)
120: ```
121: 
122: Function values can be assigned to bindings, passed as arguments, returned, and called.
123: 
124: ### Varargs
125: 
126: Varargs use a named packed argument array.
127: 
128: ```c
129: print_all := function(...args) {
130:     for i := 0; i < length(args); i++ {
131:         print(args[i])
132:     }
133: }
134: 
135: print_all("sword", 10, true)
136: ```
137: 
138: `...args` packs all remaining arguments into an array named `args`.
139: 
140: The name is user-chosen.
141: 
142: ```c
143: log := function(prefix, ...values) {
144:     print(prefix, values[0])
145: }
146: ```
147: 
148: The vararg parameter must be the final parameter.
149: 
150: ### Loops
151: 
152: Kiln uses Go/Odin-style `for` forms.
153: 
154: Infinite loop:
155: 
156: ```c
157: for {
158:     print("tick")
159: }
160: ```
161: 
162: Condition loop:
163: 
164: ```c
165: for active {
166:     update()
167: }
168: ```
169: 
170: Three-part loop:
171: 
172: ```c
173: for i := 0; i < 10; i++ {
174:     print(i)
175: }
176: ```
177: 
178: ### Length
179: 
180: `length(value)` returns the length of supported container/string values.
181: 
182: ```c
183: count := length(items)
184: ```
185: 
186: 
187: 
188: ---
189: 
190: # RULE NOTES
191: 
192: Bindings
193:     := declares a new binding in the current scope.
194:     Same-scope redeclaration is an error.
195:     = assigns an existing binding.
196:     Assignment to an unbound name is an error.
197: 
198: Dot access
199:     a.b is exactly a["b"].
200:     The left operand must be a map.
201:     Non-map dot access is an error.
202: 
203: Equality
204:     nil, bool, number, string compare by value.
205:     int and float compare by numeric value.
206:     arrays, maps, and functions compare by identity.
207:     Deep/container equality is a library function, not ==.
208: 
209: Numbers
210:     int and float are numeric subtypes.
211:     int literals have no decimal point.
212:     float literals have decimal point or exponent form, if you choose that.
213:     int op int stays int except division.
214:     mixed numeric ops promote to float.
215:     numeric comparisons compare mathematical value.
216: 
217: `,` separates values
218: `;` separates clauses/statements

(End of file - total 218 lines)
</content>

<path>C:\dev\kilnvm_dev\dev\PROJECT-DIRECTION.md</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: # Project Direction
2: 
3: Kiln is moving toward a two-part architecture:
4: 
5: ```txt
6: [SOURCE] -> compiler -> [BYTECODE] -> vm
7: ```
8: 
9: The VM remains the runtime substrate. The compiler is responsible for turning
10: source text into runnable VM data.
11: 
12: ## Compiler Direction
13: 
14: The compiler owns source-facing work:
15: 
16: ```txt
17: source text
18: tokens
19: parsing
20: name/local handling
21: slot assignment
22: bytecode emission
23: Proto construction
24: ```
25: 
26: The current plan is direct parser-to-bytecode, not a persistent AST pipeline.
27: Parser functions consume tokens and emit bytecode as they recognize source
28: forms.
29: 
30: No AST does not mean token soup. Source structure lives in parser functions:
31: 
32: ```txt
33: parse_decl
34: parse_if
35: parse_for
36: parse_return
37: parse_block
38: parse_expr
39: parse_call
40: ```
41: 
42: Each function owns one source form and emits the corresponding bytecode pattern.
43: 
44: Expected shape:
45: 
46: ```txt
47: lexer
48:     source text -> tokens
49: 
50: parser/compiler
51:     parse statements and expressions
52:     manage locals/scopes
53:     call bytecode emitters
54:     patch jumps
55:     finish Proto data
56: ```
57: 
58: This is in the same broad family as Lua/Wren-style small bytecode compilers:
59: recursive-descent statement parsing, expression parsing with precedence later,
60: and bytecode emitted during parse.
61: 
62: ## Compiler State
63: 
64: The parser/compiler state is the main compiler data model.
65: 
66: Early state can stay small:
67: 
68: ```txt
69: tokens
70: current token index
71: current proto/emitter state
72: locals: name -> slot
73: next local slot
74: ```
75: 
76: Later, only when needed, it can grow:
77: 
78: ```txt
79: scope depth or block stack
80: temporary slot cursor
81: loop break/continue patch lists
82: current function/proto context
83: source positions for errors
84: ```
85: 
86: Do not add those until a parse path needs them.
87: 
88: ## Expression Strategy
89: 
90: The first expression parser can be deliberately small.
91: 
92: Initial expression support:
93: 
94: ```txt
95: primary values
96: names
97: calls
98: one simple binary operator
99: ```
100: 
101: When expressions need real precedence, use Pratt parsing or precedence
102: climbing. That keeps expressions structured without introducing a persistent
103: AST layer.
104: 
105: ## VM
106: 
107: The VM owns runtime execution:
108: 
109: ```txt
110: Value and heap object model
111: opcode definitions
112: instruction layouts
113: Proto
114: native/proto call behavior
115: slots and call frames
116: global namespace table
117: bytecode execution
118: ```
119: 
120: The VM should not know about source text, tokens, parser state, or compiler
121: policy.
122: 
123: ## Near-Term Path
124: 
125: Next useful slice:
126: 
127: ```txt
128: 1. Keep VM stable.
129: 2. Keep the current bytecode emitter/proto builder.
130: 3. Build a small lexer for the known Kiln surface.
131: 4. Build a tiny direct parser/compiler for:
132: 
133:        x := 10
134:        y := 20
135:        print(x + y)
136: 
137: 5. Expand expression parsing and control flow only after that works.
138: ```
139: 
140: The first parser does not need a full precedence system. It can start with
141: primary expressions, calls, declarations, and one simple binary operator, then
142: grow into Pratt or precedence-climbing parsing when needed.
143: 
144: ## Future Direction
145: 
146: Disassembly can come back as tooling once the compiler/VM shape settles.
147: 
148: Proposed output format:
149: 
150: ```txt
151: .kasm
152: ```
153: 
154: The disassembler should render completed VM proto data as readable Kiln assembly:
155: 
156: ```txt
157: proto print_branch
158: params: 0
159: slots:  5
160: 
161: CONST POOL
162: 0: "print"
163: 1: 10
164: 
165: BYTECODE
166: 00 GET_GLOBAL  S0  B0       | print
167: 01 LOAD_CONST  S1  C1       | 10
168: 02 CALL        S0   1   0
169: 03 RETURN      S0   0
170: ```
171: 
172: ## Boundary Rule
173: 
174: The dependency direction stays:
175: 
176: ```txt
177: compiler -> vm
178: ```
179: 
180: Never:
181: 
182: ```txt
183: vm -> compiler
184: ```
185: 
186: The compiler can import VM types and emit VM bytecode. The VM executes completed
187: VM data.

(End of file - total 187 lines)
</content>

<content>
1: # Kiln Maps Spec
2: 
3: Map semantics and VM contract for Kiln v0.
4: 
5: This document is map-specific.  
6: `dev/VM-SPEC.md` remains the canonical global VM spec.
7: 
8: ## Scope
9: 
10: This spec defines:
11: 
12: - map runtime object shape
13: - key/value semantics
14: - map opcode contracts
15: - error behavior
16: 
17: This spec does not define:
18: 
19: - map iteration protocol
20: - non-string keys
21: - map literals/lowering strategy
22: 
23: ## Map Model
24: 
25: Kiln maps are dynamic string-keyed key/value stores.
26: 
27: Runtime object:
28: 
29: ```odin
30: MapObject :: struct {
31:     header: Object,   // kind == .MAP
32:     items:  map[string]Value,
33: }
34: ```
35: 
36: `ObjectKind` gains:
37: 
38: ```txt
39: MAP
40: ```
41: 
42: ## Key Semantics
43: 
44: Map keys are string-only at VM level.
45: 
46: For `MAP_GET` and `MAP_SET`:
47: 
48: - key operand must be a `Value` holding `^Object`
49: - object kind must be `STRING`
50: - VM extracts `StringObject.data` and uses that `string` as the backing key
51: 
52: Key equality is by string text, not object identity.
53: 
54: So two different `StringObject` instances with equal text address the same map entry.
55: 
56: ## Value Semantics
57: 
58: Map values use `Value`, with one policy rule:
59: 
60: - setting a key to `nil` deletes the key
61: 
62: Therefore:
63: 
64: - maps do not retain bindings with `nil` values
65: - lookup miss and deleted key both read as `nil`
66: 
67: ## Opcode Set
68: 
69: Minimal map opcodes:
70: 
71: - `NEW_MAP`
72: - `MAP_LEN`
73: - `MAP_GET`
74: - `MAP_SET`
75: 
76: No `MAP_DELETE` opcode in v0.
77: 
78: Deletion is expressed as `MAP_SET` with `nil` value.
79: 
80: ## Opcode Contracts
81: 
82: ### NEW_MAP
83: 
84: ```txt
85: layout: ABx
86: A = dst
87: B = reserved (must be encoded as 0 for now)
88: ```
89: 
90: Semantics:
91: 
92: ```txt
93: slot[A] = new empty map object
94: ```
95: 
96: ### MAP_LEN
97: 
98: ```txt
99: layout: ABx
100: A = dst
101: B = src_map
102: ```
103: 
104: Semantics:
105: 
106: ```txt
107: slot[A] = i64(number of live bindings in map)
108: ```
109: 
110: ### MAP_GET
111: 
112: ```txt
113: layout: ABC
114: A = dst
115: B = src_map
116: C = key
117: ```
118: 
119: Semantics:
120: 
121: ```txt
122: if key exists:
123:     slot[A] = map[key_text]
124: else:
125:     slot[A] = nil
126: ```
127: 
128: ### MAP_SET
129: 
130: ```txt
131: layout: ABC
132: A = dst_map
133: B = key
134: C = src
135: ```
136: 
137: Semantics:
138: 
139: ```txt
140: value := slot[C]
141: 
142: if value == nil:
143:     delete key_text from map      // no-op if missing
144: else:
145:     map[key_text] = value         // insert or update
146: ```
147: 
148: ## Error Policy
149: 
150: Runtime panic for:
151: 
152: - non-map operand where map is required
153: - key operand that is not a `STRING` object
154: 
155: Not an error:
156: 
157: - `MAP_GET` miss (`nil` result)
158: - delete missing key via `MAP_SET ... nil` (no-op)
159: 
160: ## Derived Behavior
161: 
162: - upsert semantics: same opcode handles insert and update
163: - delete semantics are explicit in value policy (`nil` means remove binding)
164: - no missing-vs-present-nil ambiguity
165: 
166: ## Deferred
167: 
168: Keep out of v0 map surface:
169: 
170: - `MAP_HAS`
171: - `MAP_DELETE` opcode
172: - map iteration opcodes
173: - non-string key support
174: 

(End of file - total 174 lines)
</content>

**Tool Call: dev\LANG-IDEAS.md**
Status: Completed

<path>C:\dev\kilnvm_dev\dev\LANG-IDEAS.md</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: # Lang Ideas
2: 
3: Non-canonical language-surface notes.  
4: `VM-SPEC.md` defines current runtime behavior.
5: 
6: ## Language Identity
7: 
8: Target style:
9: 
10: - small procedural scripting language
11: - explicit runtime model
12: - first-class callable values
13: - no hidden object model behaviors
14: 
15: Hard constraints currently assumed:
16: 
17: - no closures/upvalues
18: - no nested function declarations
19: - no classes/methods
20: - no operator overloading/metatable dispatch
21: - no macros
22: 
23: ## Multi-Return Surface Direction
24: 
25: VM already supports fixed call-site arity (`CALL ... requested_results`).
26: 
27: Current preferred surface:
28: 
29: - plain assignment captures first result
30: - explicit full capture via `pack(...)`
31: - explicit expansion via `unpack(...)`
32: 
33: This keeps multi-result behavior explicit and avoids implicit open-result propagation rules.
34: 
35: ## Modules / Namespaces (Open)
36: 
37: 
38: 
39: ## Kiln varargs
40: 
41: Kiln varargs use a named parameter.
42: 
43: ```kiln
44: log : fn(prefix, ...args)
45:     print(prefix)
46:     print(args[0])
47: end
48: ````
49: 
50: `args` is user-defined. It could be `args`, `values`, `items`, or any valid parameter name.
51: 
52: ```kiln
53: log : fn(prefix, ...args)
54:     print(args[0])
55: end
56: ```
57: 
58: The `...name` form means:
59: 
60: ```txt
61: pack all remaining arguments into an array and bind that array to name
62: ```
63: 
64: So:
65: 
66: ```kiln
67: log("hit", 10, 20, 30)
68: ```
69: 
70: enters the function as:
71: 
72: ```txt
73: prefix = "hit"
74: args   = [10, 20, 30]
75: ```
76: 
77: If no extra arguments are passed, the args parameter is an empty array.
78: 
79: ```kiln
80: log("hit")
81: ```
82: 
83: ```txt
84: prefix = "hit"
85: args   = []
86: ```
87: 
88: Rules:
89: 
90: * `...name` is only valid as the final parameter.
91: * the args parameter is an array.
92: * extra arguments are packed into that array.
93: * fixed parameters bind normally before the args array is created.
94: 
95: ```
96: 

(End of file - total 96 lines)
</content>

**Tool Call: dev\STRUCT-IMPL-IDEAS.md**
Status: Completed

<path>C:\dev\kilnvm_dev\dev\STRUCT-IMPL-IDEAS.md</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: # Struct Impl Ideas
2: 
3: Current direction for kiln structs.
4: 
5: This replaces the older type-like struct direction. The goal is still fixed-field
6: records with fast field access, but the source model is now more dynamic-language
7: oriented:
8: 
9: - `struct { ... }` creates an immediate fixed-field struct value.
10: - `struct def { ... }` creates a reusable struct definition / constructor.
11: - `Name { ... }` constructs a struct instance from a struct definition.
12: 
13: Structs are not classes. They do not have methods, inheritance, hidden dispatch,
14: metatables, or operator overloading.
15: 
16: ## Goal
17: 
18: Structs exist to provide a stricter alternative to maps for record-like data.
19: 
20: They exist for:
21: 
22: - fixed field names
23: - unknown-field errors
24: - field access by offset
25: - predictable construction from defaults
26: - reusable constructor definitions when needed
27: - lightweight runtime validation through `kind(...)`
28: 
29: The useful part is not dot syntax by itself. The useful part is that a struct has
30: a known field layout, so field names can be resolved to field indexes instead of
31: performing dynamic string-key map lookup.
32: 
33: Conceptual split:
34: 
35: ```txt
36: array
37:     ordered indexed sequence
38: 
39: map
40:     open dynamic key/value store
41: 
42: struct
43:     immediate fixed-field value
44: 
45: struct def
46:     reusable fixed-field constructor/default definition
47: ```
48: 
49: ## Source Surface
50: 
51: Immediate one-off struct value:
52: 
53: ```go
54: state := struct {
55:     score := 0,
56:     paused := false,
57: }
58: ```
59: 
60: Reusable struct definition:
61: 
62: ```go
63: Entity := struct def {
64:     name := "",
65:     hp := 100,
66:     inventory := [],
67: }
68: ```
69: 
70: Construct from a struct definition:
71: 
72: ```go
73: goblin := Entity {
74:     name = "goblin",
75: }
76: ```
77: 
78: The `struct` form creates a value now.
79: 
80: The `struct def` form creates a constructable definition. It stores the field
81: layout and default expressions. Constructing from it evaluates defaults fresh,
82: then applies overrides.
83: 
84: The constructor override block uses `=` because it writes values into the new
85: instance.
86: 
87: The struct body uses `:=` because it declares fields and their defaults.
88: 
89: ## Field Forms
90: 
91: Supported field forms:
92: 
93: ```go
94: name,
95: name := expr,
96: ```
97: 
98: A bare field defaults to `nil`:
99: 
100: ```go
101: Entity := struct def {
102:     name,
103:     hp,
104: }
105: ```
106: 
107: is equivalent to:
108: 
109: ```go
110: Entity := struct def {
111:     name := nil,
112:     hp := nil,
113: }
114: ```
115: 
116: Mixed fields are allowed:
117: 
118: ```go
119: Entity := struct def {
120:     name := "",
121:     hp := 100,
122:     target,
123: }
124: ```
125: 
126: Commas separate fields. A trailing comma is allowed.
127: 
128: ## Semantics
129: 
130: Struct fields are fixed.
131: 
132: Unknown field access or assignment is an error:
133: 
134: ```go
135: goblin.hp = 50    // ok
136: goblin.hhp = 50   // error: unknown field
137: ```
138: 
139: Struct field values are dynamic. MVP structs do not enforce field value types.
140: 
141: This is valid in the MVP model:
142: 
143: ```go
144: goblin.hp = "dead"
145: ```
146: 
147: That may be undesirable user code, but it is not a struct semantic error.
148: Structs solve shape discipline first:
149: 
150: ```txt
151: this field exists / this field does not exist
152: ```
153: 
154: not full runtime field type contracts.
155: 
156: Future optional runtime field contracts may be added later, but they are not part
157: of this MVP design.
158: 
159: ## Defaults
160: 
161: For `struct def`, default expressions are evaluated each time an instance is
162: constructed.
163: 
164: ```go
165: Entity := struct def {
166:     name := "",
167:     inventory := [],
168: }
169: 
170: a := Entity {}
171: b := Entity {}
172: ```
173: 
174: `a.inventory` and `b.inventory` are separate arrays because `[]` is evaluated for
175: each construction.
176: 
177: This avoids the mutable-default footgun that appears if a reusable constructor is
178: just a live prototype value cloned shallowly.
179: 
180: For immediate `struct`, field expressions are evaluated immediately because the
181: struct value is created immediately.
182: 
183: ```go
184: state := struct {
185:     score := 0,
186:     inventory := [],
187: }
188: ```
189: 
190: ## Construction
191: 
192: A struct definition is constructable:
193: 
194: ```go
195: Entity := struct def {
196:     name := "",
197:     hp := 100,
198: }
199: 
200: e := Entity {}
201: ```
202: 
203: Constructor overrides must name existing fields:
204: 
205: ```go
206: e := Entity {
207:     name = "goblin",
208: }
209: ```
210: 
211: Extra fields are errors:
212: 
213: ```go
214: e := Entity {
215:     speed = 10, // error: unknown field
216: }
217: ```
218: 
219: Duplicate constructor fields are errors:
220: 
221: ```go
222: e := Entity {
223:     hp = 10,
224:     hp = 20, // error: duplicate field
225: }
226: ```
227: 
228: Omitted fields use defaults.
229: 
230: Immediate struct values are not constructable:
231: 
232: ```go
233: state := struct {
234:     score := 0,
235: }
236: 
237: other := state {} // error: expected struct def
238: ```
239: 
240: ## `type` and `kind`
241: 
242: `type(value)` returns the broad runtime category.
243: 
244: Expected struct-related results:
245: 
246: ```go
247: type(Entity) // "struct_def"
248: type(goblin) // "struct"
249: type(state)  // "struct"
250: ```
251: 
252: `kind(value)` returns a lightweight label for struct values and struct defs.
253: 
254: The label is captured from a direct binding name when possible:
255: 
256: ```go
257: Entity := struct def {
258:     name := "",
259:     hp := 100,
260: }
261: 
262: goblin := Entity {}
263: 
264: kind(Entity) // "Entity"
265: kind(goblin) // "Entity"
266: ```
267: 
268: For immediate structs:
269: 
270: ```go
271: state := struct {
272:     score := 0,
273: }
274: 
275: kind(state) // "state"
276: ```
277: 
278: For anonymous inline expression use, `kind(...)` may return `nil`:
279: 
280: ```go
281: items.push(struct { x := 0, })
282: 
283: kind(items[0]) // nil
284: ```
285: 
286: `kind` is a label, not a deep static type identity system.
287: 
288: Typical validation:
289: 
290: ```go
291: if kind(entity) != "Entity" {
292:     error("expected Entity")
293: }
294: ```
295: 
296: This is intended as a simple replacement for common Lua-style table validation,
297: not as a full type checker.
298: 
299: ## Runtime Model
300: 
301: Use two runtime concepts:
302: 
303: ```txt
304: StructDefObject
305:     reusable field layout and default expressions
306: 
307: StructObject
308:     one fixed-field struct value
309: ```
310: 
311: Likely first-pass shape:
312: 
313: ```odin
314: StructDefObject :: struct {
315:     header:      Object, // must be first
316:     kind:        string,
317:     field_names: []string,
318:     defaults:    []DefaultExpr, // or lowered default construction data
319: }
320: 
321: StructObject :: struct {
322:     header: Object, // must be first
323:     def:    ^StructDefObject,
324:     fields: []Value,
325: }
326: ```
327: 
328: Immediate `struct { ... }` can still create an internal struct definition object
329: for its field layout, then create a `StructObject` from it. That definition is
330: not user-constructable unless exposed through a `struct def` value.
331: 
332: A first-pass implementation can use exact-size field storage:
333: 
334: ```txt
335: fields = make([]Value, field_count)
336: ```
337: 
338: This keeps storage proportional to the actual struct while still allowing direct
339: field-index access:
340: 
341: ```txt
342: fields[field_index]
343: ```
344: 
345: ## Field Lookup
346: 
347: Dynamic map access:
348: 
349: ```txt
350: value["hp"]
351: hash key, probe, compare, load value
352: ```
353: 
354: Struct field access:
355: 
356: ```txt
357: value.fields[field_index]
358: load known field offset
359: ```
360: 
361: Hot struct field access should not search field names. Field names are for
362: compiler resolution, construction validation, debug output, and possible later
363: reflection.
364: 
365: If the compiler knows the struct layout at compile time, dot access can lower to
366: field-index ops.
367: 
368: ```go
369: goblin.hp
370: ```
371: 
372: can lower to:
373: 
374: ```txt
375: STRUCT_GET dst, goblin_slot, field_index
376: ```
377: 
378: and:
379: 
380: ```go
381: goblin.hp = 10
382: ```
383: 
384: can lower to:
385: 
386: ```txt
387: STRUCT_SET goblin_slot, value_slot, field_index
388: ```
389: 
390: Runtime checks:
391: 
392: - value must be a `struct`
393: - field index must be in bounds
394: - constructor target must be a `struct_def`
395: - constructor fields must exist
396: - duplicate constructor fields are errors
397: 
398: ## Bytecode Direction
399: 
400: Likely first opcode set:
401: 
402: ```txt
403: NEW_STRUCT        create struct instance from StructDefObject
404: STRUCT_GET        read field by offset
405: STRUCT_SET        write field by offset
406: ```
407: 
408: For immediate struct values, either:
409: 
410: ```txt
411: NEW_STRUCT_IMMEDIATE
412: ```
413: 
414: or the compiler can lower through an internal generated `StructDefObject` plus
415: `NEW_STRUCT`.
416: 
417: Construction from a named def:
418: 
419: ```go
420: Entity := struct def {
421:     name := "",
422:     hp := 100,
423: }
424: 
425: goblin := Entity {
426:     name = "goblin",
427: }
428: ```
429: 
430: rough lowering:
431: 
432: ```txt
433: LOAD_STRUCT_DEF   tmp_def, EntityDef
434: NEW_STRUCT        dst, tmp_def       // evaluates defaults fresh
435: LOAD_CONST        tmp, "goblin"
436: STRUCT_SET        dst, tmp, field_name_index(name)
437: ```
438: 
439: Default evaluation needs its own lowering strategy. Simple constants can be
440: stored directly. Container defaults like `[]`, `map { ... }`, or `struct { ... }`
441: should be evaluated per construction, not shared as one mutable default value.
442: 
443: ## Relationship To Maps
444: 
445: Maps and structs are separate containers.
446: 
447: Maps are open dynamic key/value stores:
448: 
449: ```go
450: weights := map {
451:     "goblin": 10,
452:     "orc": 20,
453: }
454: ```
455: 
456: Structs are closed fixed-field records:
457: 
458: ```go
459: state := struct {
460:     score := 0,
461:     paused := false,
462: }
463: ```
464: 
465: A map is appropriate when keys are dynamic, data-shaped, sparse, user-provided,
466: or not known up front.
467: 
468: A struct is appropriate when the field set is known and accidental extra/missing
469: fields should be treated as errors.
470: 
471: There is no `map def` in the MVP design. Reusable fixed schemas are the job of
472: `struct def`.
473: 
474: ## Relationship To Binding Tables
475: 
476: `BindingTable` is namespace storage:
477: 
478: ```txt
479: name -> runtime value
480: ```
481: 
482: `StructDefObject` is field layout and default construction data:
483: 
484: ```txt
485: field name -> field offset/default
486: ```
487: 
488: Do not reuse `BindingTable` for struct fields unless a real need appears. Plain
489: field-name storage plus field count should be enough.
490: 
491: ## Future Optional Runtime Contracts
492: 
493: A future extension may add opt-in runtime contracts:
494: 
495: ```go
496: state := struct {
497:     score: int = 0,
498:     paused: bool = false,
499: }
500: ```
501: 
502: That is not part of the MVP struct design.
503: 
504: MVP structs should stay focused on:
505: 
506: - fixed fields
507: - defaults
508: - constructor definitions
509: - `kind` labels
510: - offset field access
511: 
512: ## Later Storage Optimization
513: 
514: Exact-size `[]Value` fields are the clean first implementation.
515: 
516: Later, struct instances could use trailing field storage:
517: 
518: ```txt
519: [StructObject header/def][Value field 0][Value field 1][Value field 2]
520: ```
521: 
522: That gives:
523: 
524: - one allocation per struct instance
525: - exact field count storage
526: - no separate field slice allocation
527: - no fixed max-field waste per instance
528: 
529: The cost is manual allocation/layout work:
530: 
531: - total allocation size
532: - alignment of trailing `Value` storage
533: - producing a field slice/view over trailing memory
534: - object size/traversal concerns for future GC/freeing
535: 
536: This should stay an internal storage change. Source semantics and bytecode-level
537: struct behavior should remain unchanged.

(End of file - total 537 lines)
</content>

**Tool Call: dev\TYPE-TAGS-FUTURE.md**
Status: Completed

<path>C:\dev\kilnvm_dev\dev\TYPE-TAGS-FUTURE.md</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
1: # Type Tags / Runtime Contracts
2: 
3: Possible future direction for Kiln runtime validation.
4: 
5: This is not part of the first struct implementation. For now, Kiln should stay simple:
6: use normal runtime checks such as `type(value)` and ordinary assertions.
7: 
8: ## Current Direction
9: 
10: Kiln values are dynamic.
11: 
12: A normal binding can hold any value:
13: 
14: ```kiln
15: x := 10
16: x = "hello"
17: ```
18: 
19: For now, validation should be explicit and ordinary:
20: 
21: ```kiln
22: assert(type(amount) == "int", "expected int")
23: assert(type(name) == "str", "expected str")
24: ```
25: 
26: A small helper could make this less noisy:
27: 
28: ```kiln
29: assert_type(amount, "int")
30: assert_type(name, "str")
31: ```
32: 
33: For struct-like validation, `kind(value)` can be used when the value came from a
34: named `struct def`:
35: 
36: ```kiln
37: Entity := struct def {
38:     name := "",
39:     hp := 100,
40: }
41: 
42: goblin := Entity {
43:     name = "goblin",
44: }
45: 
46: assert(type(goblin) == "struct", "expected struct")
47: assert(kind(goblin) == "Entity", "expected Entity")
48: ```
49: 
50: This keeps validation visible and avoids pulling a type-contract system into the
51: first language slice.
52: 
53: ## Possible Future Syntax
54: 
55: A later runtime-contract feature could use tags on bindings, fields, and
56: parameters:
57: 
58: ```kiln
59: score: int = 0
60: name: str = "goblin"
61: ```
62: 
63: The tag would be a runtime contract, not a static type.
64: 
65: ```kiln
66: score: int = 0
67: score = 10      // ok
68: score = "bad"   // runtime error
69: ```
70: 
71: This is not type inference, generics, or compile-time checking. It is just a
72: slot-level runtime guard.
73: 
74: ## Possible Struct Field Contracts
75: 
76: If type tags are added later, they could compose with `struct` and `struct def`:
77: 
78: ```kiln
79: Entity := struct def {
80:     name: str = "",
81:     hp: int = 100,
82: }
83: ```
84: 
85: This would mean:
86: 
87: ```txt
88: name defaults to "" and later non-nil assignments must be str
89: hp defaults to 100 and later non-nil assignments must be int
90: ```
91: 
92: The first struct system does not need this. Plain structs still provide value:
93: 
94: ```kiln
95: Entity := struct def {
96:     name := "",
97:     hp := 100,
98: }
99: ```
100: 
101: Plain structs enforce field existence, not field value type.
102: 
103: ```kiln
104: goblin := Entity{}
105: goblin.hp = "dead"  // allowed in the untagged struct model
106: goblin.hhp = 10     // error: unknown field
107: ```
108: 
109: ## Possible Parameter Contracts
110: 
111: Runtime contracts could also apply to function parameters later:
112: 
113: ```kiln
114: damage := function(entity: struct, amount: int) {
115:     entity.hp = entity.hp - amount
116: }
117: ```
118: 
119: This would check arguments at call time.
120: 
121: This should stay optional. Kiln should not require every function to be tagged.
122: Tags are for places where validation is useful.
123: 
124: ## Possible Union Contracts
125: 
126: A small union form may be useful later:
127: 
128: ```kiln
129: amount: int | float = 0
130: maybe_name: str | nil = nil
131: ```
132: 
133: This should be considered future work. It should not be required for the first
134: runtime-contract feature.
135: 
136: ## Possible Contracted Maps
137: 
138: Kiln maps are string-keyed dynamic stores. A possible future extension could add
139: runtime contracts for map values only:
140: 
141: ```kiln
142: scores := int map{
143:     "goblin": 10,
144:     "orc": 20,
145: }
146: ```
147: 
148: This would mean:
149: 
150: ```txt
151: keys are still strings
152: missing keys still read nil
153: assigning nil still deletes/removes the key
154: non-nil assigned values must match the map value contract
155: ```
156: 
157: For struct values, the contract should only accept named `struct def` values:
158: 
159: ```kiln
160: Player := struct def {
161:     name := "",
162:     hp := 100,
163: }
164: 
165: players := Player map{}
166: 
167: players["jacob"] = Player {
168:     name = "Jacob",
169: }
170: 
171: players["orc"] = Enemy{} // runtime error if Enemy has a different kind
172: players["jacob"] = nil   // deletes key
173: ```
174: 
175: `Player map{}` does not mean generic static typing. It means the map has a
176: runtime value guard:
177: 
178: ```txt
179: non-nil values assigned to this map must be structs with kind "Player"
180: ```
181: 
182: This feature belongs in the same future bucket as type tags. It should not be
183: part of the first map or struct implementation.
184: 
185: Avoid early deep type machinery:
186: 
187: ```txt
188: array<int>
189: map<str, int>
190: generic T
191: interfaces / traits
192: compile-time proofs
193: ```
194: 
195: Those move Kiln toward a real type system. Runtime tags should stay small,
196: local, and explicit.
197: 
198: ## Suggested Builtins For Now
199: 
200: Before type tags exist, these are enough:
201: 
202: ```kiln
203: type(value)        // broad runtime type string
204: kind(value)        // struct kind label string, if any
205: assert(condition, message)
206: assert_type(value, expected_type)
207: ```
208: 
209: Possible `assert_type` behavior:
210: 
211: ```kiln
212: assert_type(value, "int")
213: assert_type(value, "str")
214: assert_type(value, "array")
215: assert_type(value, "map")
216: assert_type(value, "struct")
217: ```
218: 
219: Possible `assert_kind` helper:
220: 
221: ```kiln
222: assert_kind(value, "Entity")
223: ```
224: 
225: These helpers are ordinary runtime checks. They do not require new syntax.
226: 
227: ## Design Line
228: 
229: MVP Kiln should keep:
230: 
231: ```txt
232: bindings dynamic
233: struct fields dynamic
234: validation explicit
235: ```
236: 
237: Future Kiln may add:
238: 
239: ```txt
240: optional runtime contracts with `name: contract = value`
241: ```
242: 
243: But these contracts should remain runtime guards, not a static type system.

(End of file - total 243 lines)
</content>

<content>
1: # Validated Bring-Up Surface
2: 
3: Loose checklist of VM features that have been manually exercised during bring-up.
4: 
5: - value variants: `nil`, `bool`, `i64`, `f64`, object pointer values
6: - object kinds: `STRING`, `PROTO_FUNCTION`, `NATIVE_FUNCTION`, `ARRAY`, `MAP`
7: - `LOAD_CONST`
8: - `LOAD_FUNC` historical path validated before child-proto refactor
9: - immediate load ops: `LOAD_NIL`, `LOAD_TRUE`, `LOAD_FALSE`
10: - arithmetic ops: `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `NEG` (including int/float mixing)
11: - comparison/boolean ops: `EQUAL`, `LESS`, `LESS_OR_EQUAL`, `NOT`
12: - control flow ops: `JUMP`, `JUMP_FALSE` (falsey semantics), `HALT`
13: - call/return path:
14:   - `CALL` to native function objects
15:   - `CALL` to proto function objects
16:   - `RETURN` produced/requested result shaping
17:   - top-level `RETURN` host value behavior
18: - global binding path: `BindingTable`, `BindingId`, `GET_GLOBAL`, `SET_GLOBAL`
19: - native print integration via global lookup and native call
20: - bytecode generation bring-up:
21:   - `compiler` package depends on `vm`
22: - `gen.begin_proto`
23:   - `gen.store_const_int`
24:   - `gen.emit_load_const`
25:   - `gen.emit_add`
26:   - `gen.emit_return`
27:   - `gen.next_inst_index`
28:   - `gen.emit_jump`
29:   - `gen.emit_jump_false`
30:   - `gen.patch_jump`
31: - `gen.end_proto`
32:   - generated proto runs through VM and returns host value
33: - array construction/query/mutation:
34:   - `NEW_ARRAY` (capacity operand used)
35:   - `ARRAY_LEN`
36:   - `ARRAY_GET`
37:   - `ARRAY_SET`
38:   - `ARRAY_PUSH`
39:   - `ARRAY_POP`
40: - array semantics:
41:   - dense 0-based indexing
42:   - `nil` can be stored as an array element
43:   - `ARRAY_GET` can read stored `nil`
44:   - `ARRAY_POP` can return stored `nil`
45:   - empty `ARRAY_POP` panics
46:   - nested arrays
47:   - mixed element arrays (`nil`, `i64`, `f64`, `bool`, `STRING`, nested `ARRAY`)
48:   - reference behavior for nested arrays through outer container
49: - array/native-print rendering path:
50:   - primitive values
51:   - strings
52:   - arrays (including nested arrays)
53: - map surface:
54:   - `ObjectKind.MAP`
55:   - `MapObject` with `map[string]Value` storage
56:   - `NEW_MAP`
57:   - `MAP_LEN`
58:   - `MAP_GET`
59:   - `MAP_SET`
60:   - string keys compare by text through `StringObject.data`
61:   - string value insert
62:   - integer value insert
63:   - float value insert
64:   - bool value insert
65:   - existing key update
66:   - missing key reads as `nil`
67:   - setting nil deletes key
68:   - deleting missing key is a no-op
69:   - length reflects stored entries
70:   - false map values stay stored but fail truthiness branches
71:   - map value can be read, branched on, updated, deleted, and read again
72:   - `MAP` object value passed through native `print`
73:   - whole-map native print renders entries in unspecified order
74:   - map values render through native print as strings, numbers, bools, and nil
75: - failure paths:
76:   - `ARRAY_GET` out-of-bounds panics
77:   - `ARRAY_SET` out-of-bounds panics
78:   - `ARRAY_POP` on empty panics
79:   - `ARRAY_GET` with non-`i64` index panics
80: 
81: Previous bytecode/builtin manual run:
82: 
83: ```txt
84: [10, nil, 30]
85: {hp: 99, alive: true, name: "kiln"}
86: 10
87: 3
88: 3
89: 4
90: "nil"
91: "bool"
92: "int"
93: "float"
94: "string"
95: "array"
96: "map"
97: "function"
98: "123"
99: "[10, nil, 30]"
100: 123
101: 3.5
102: 42
103: 4.5
104: nil
105: builtin surface: 3
106: ```
107: 
108: The map line is unordered by design.
109: 
110: Notes:
111: 
112: - This is manual bring-up validation, not a formal automated test suite.
113: - `src/main.odin` is currently a scanner dump harness; the bytecode/builtin harness above was removed from `main` after being noted here.
114: - Current child-proto `LOAD_FUNC` path compiles but still needs a source-level function test.
115: 

(End of file - total 115 lines)
</content>
